top_categories.workout_context: Race-pace efforts that mimic UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before October.
At mile 60 of 87, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
5,200ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
60ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou. Train it until it feels boring.
UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 87 miles.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 5,200ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 87 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
At UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Short, max efforts are unavoidable. Punchy climbs, gap closures, attacks on mixed terrain. If you haven't trained them, you'll crack.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 87 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Tempo is UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 87 miles on mixed terrain. Train it until it's automatic.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for UCI Gran Fondo Hangzhou’s 87 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Race Simulation, Durability, and VO2max to prepare you for 87 miles of mixed terrain in Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China.
